title: Warsaw, Poland bike touring Vlog July 24ish - Romania, Moldova, Ukraine, Poland

transcript.text: Okay, here's a quick video blog of my travel. I'm in Warsaw, Poland.
Since the last video blog I did, it was on the train between Sofia, Bulgaria and Bucharest, Romania.
I spent a day in Bucharest, Romania, an entire day, and that was really fun.
I did a really thorough video shoot of Bucharest, even more than Sofia.
I'm starting to really get into these video shoots of cities, kind of make it seem like you're there when you take the video.
And then the next day I rode my bike. I was going to ride my bike to Chisinau, and I rode my bike.
I started riding at like 11.30, and it rained three times really hard, and I got one of my MP3 players wet.
Not the one that was on my jersey. It wasn't even protected at all. It didn't get wet.
One inside my bag, but it was like touching the side, so the water seeped through and grabbed it and sucked it all in.
And it's still wet like a week later, so hopefully it'll be fine.
But anyways, I rode like 140 miles that day, and that was actually my biggest day yet.
And I camped out in like some field, and there were so many mosquitoes, like I was eaten alive, even while setting up my tent.
But once I got my tent, I was okay.
But then it like, that day I was having trouble.
It was raining, I guess, and particles were getting in my contacts, and my left eye was hurting, but then it was okay.
But then like that morning at 4 o'clock in the morning, my left, my right eye started hurting, so I had to take my contact out.
And then the next morning, I took the left contact out and put my glasses on, and I was riding down the road.
And then I felt fine.
My right eye was a little bit fuzzy, but I felt fine.
But then all of a sudden, as I was rolling into this big town called Jelani, my right eye started suddenly hurting so bad that it fused itself shut.
And it fused the left eye shut, too, so I couldn't see at all.
So I kind of like made my way, kind of like opening my left eye just a little bit to this hotel.
And I stayed at a hotel that night.
And then all night, until like 10 or something, I was like in the most pain I'd ever been in my entire life.
My right eye was, because like, I just, even with my eye closed and trying to keep my eye still, it was still incredibly painful.
And sometimes I'd open my eye, but then closing my eye would just be so painful.
I got like some kind of corneal abrasion.
And like, I even, I got some pain reliever drops, one on pharmacy, but it didn't do me any good.
So I had to go to another pharmacy, one of the, like, the maids at the hotel had to take me by hand to a pharmacy.
And then to like an eye doctor to get the prescription, then to the pharmacy.
And I put those in, and it was still hurting.
But anyways, by morning, I was okay.
And I tried to ride, though, and I put the drops in, and I still couldn't see.
Maybe the drops, like, dilated my pupils, or my eye was still really sensitive to the air.
Like, if I was going over 10 miles an hour, I couldn't see.
So I just took the train to Chisinau that night.
And now I vowed never to ride my bike with contacts again, ever.
From now on, whenever I ride my bike, until I get LASIK, it's only on prescription glasses.
So then I was hung out at Chisinau the next day, and then I spent, and then I hung out the next day in Chisinau.
Chisinau is a really boring city.
I should have taken video with my cell phone, but I didn't think about it, just to document it.
But I'll talk about what I think about Chisinau later.
But I hung out with this American girl and this Belgian guy that night.
Then the next day, in the afternoon at like 8, I took the train to Kiev.
It was the night train.
And I hung out with these two.
It was like jam-packed, like sardines in this train, but I had my own bed to sleep in.
And I put my bike way up on top, above the second bunk.
But these, like, Russians made me drink all these, gave me six shots of the strongest vodka I've ever had in my life.
Like, the first time I took it, I was like, I thought I was going to die.
And then, like, a bottle, a glass of wine, so I threw up all over the bathroom.
But I threw up, I guess, kind of early, so I wasn't really that hungover the next morning.
And then he, like, made me drink another beer the next day.
Then I rolled into Kiev, and I got into Kiev and saw Kiev.
I should have taken more video.
I did take a little bit of video with my cell phone, but then I was starting to get used to the idea.
Even though the video from cell phone isn't good, because it, like, ch-ch-ch-ch-ch, you know.
So I hung out in Kiev.
Kiev's a really pretty city.
I guess I'll talk about that in my video when I think about Ukraine.
Big river that goes through it, and it's really hilly.
And then I rode out and stayed with the couch surfer people who I met with couch surfer.
These are an American couple, and they're really fun to hang out with.
And then the next morning, I had my train at, like, 4.30, so I hung out in Kiev and rode around and checked it out.
It's pretty city.
Although it's hard to get around, because everything's written in Cyrillic.
Um, and then I took the night train from Kiev into Warsaw.
And it was a different kind of train.
I shared a compartment with this woman.
It was a little bit more comfortable than the other train.
And then I rolled into here at, like, 9 in the morning.
Then I hung out all day here, but then I had to stay here.
Because when I was at the train station in Jelani, I, like, because I was going to try and ride, so I had my, you know, my biking clothes on.
And I took my video camera out, the new one that I got just, like, three weeks ago for 300 bucks in Greece.
And I put it on the counter, and I left it there.
Which is something that I do all the time.
It's, like, really dangerous.
That's what I do.
So, sucked.
So I didn't have a video camera.
But, um, then I rolled in here, and yesterday I bought a new video camera.
This video camera, it's a Panasonic now.
But it still doesn't have the same format as, um, you know, um, as, uh, the, um, it's something that can be edited in, uh, in, um, Movie Maker.
But I think maybe I'll start making the videos directly from, from this AVS software that I got.
I'll pay the 56 bucks, so I don't have to have that little sign thing in the middle.
But that way I won't have to use the Movie Maker at all.
But that way I'll have, I'll have to, like, see.
I don't think it has a feature where you can see how many minutes it is, because you have to keep them under 10 minutes.
But anyways, I'll figure it out, whatever.
Um, and I also got glasses, because my glasses broke when I got really drunk on the train.
And, uh, so, I, uh, that was, like, another 220 bucks.
But these are, like, good glasses that are better.
Those other ones are cheap.
These ones, I think, are more durable.
And they turn into sunglasses.
So they're, and they're healthy, and they're light, and they have, you know, they're, they stay on good.
So I think I can use them as, like, bicycling glasses.
So, anyways, I'm going to stay here again tonight.
And, uh, tomorrow morning at 7.30, I'm going to take the train into Vilnius, Lithuania.
And then it's Latvia, and then it's Estonia, and then it's Helsinki.
And once I get to Helsinki, I get to start riding.
Like, that'll probably be around the beginning of, uh, of August.
And then I can start riding.
And hopefully I'll be, I'll get, be able to experience where the sun never sets up there from northern Scandinavia.
I'm just going to ride up there as fast as I can.
And just ride around there as long as I can until I don't get the 24 hours anymore.
And then ride down south.
And, um, then I fly out on September 15th, man.
It's like a month, it's going to be like a month and a half more only.
So my, my trip's kind of coming to an end.
Okay, so that's, anyways, the update.
